Controller Area
Network
Errors, Higher Layers & Evolution
Module 5 of 6 —CAN Training Series
R E L I A B I L I T Y
Error Detection — The Five Error Types
2
Error
How it is detected
Detected by
Bit error
A transmitter reads back a bit different from the one it sent.
Transmitter
Stuff error
Six consecutive identical bits appear where stuffing should have inserted an edge.
All nodes
CRC error
The receiver's recomputed CRC disagrees with the transmitted CRC.
Receivers
Form error
A fixed
-format field (delimiter, EOF) holds an illegal value.
All nodes
ACK error
No receiver drives the ACK slot dominant, so the transmitter sees no
acknowledgement.
Transmitter
Any single detected error triggers an error frame that aborts the message — so a corrupted frame is destroyed and automatically
retransmitted.
F A U LT C O N F I N E M E N T
Error Counters & Node States
3
Two counters — the Transmit (TEC) and Receive (REC) error counters — move each node through three states, isolating a faulty node before it
can disrupt the bus.
Error Active
TEC and REC ≤ 127
Full participation; signals errors
with an active (dominant) error flag.
Error Passive
TEC or REC > 127
Still communicates but uses a
passive error flag and waits longer
between sends.
Bus-Off
TEC > 255
Node removes itself from the bus;
recovers after 128 × 11 recessive
bits.
Counters increase on errors and decrease on successful transfers, so a node that recovers can climb back toward Error Active.
A P P L I C AT I O N L AY E R
Higher-Layer Protocols
4
CAN defines how bits move, not what they mean. Higher-layer protocols add addressing, data models and services on top.
CANopen
Industrial automation; device profiles
and object dictionary (CiA 301).
SAE J1939
Heavy-duty & off-highway vehicles; 29-
bit IDs with PGNs and SPNs.
OBD-II
Mandated emissions diagnostics over
CAN (ISO 15765 / ISO-TP).
UDS (ISO 14229)
Unified Diagnostic Services for ECU
flashing and fault reading.
NMEA 2000
Marine instrumentation and navigation
networks.
DeviceNet
Factory-floor device networking built on
CAN.
E V O L U T I O N
CAN FD — Flexible Data-Rate
5
■Bosch, 2012. Standardised in ISO 11898-1:2015 to break the 1
Mbit/s and 8-byte limits.
■Up to 64 data bytes. Eight times the classic payload, cutting
protocol overhead per byte.
■Bit-rate switching (BRS). The data phase can accelerate to 8
Mbit/s while arbitration stays robust.
■Stronger CRC. Longer, more capable CRC protects the larger
frames; the FDF bit flags an FD frame.
Feature
Classic CAN
CAN FD
Max payload
8 bytes
64 bytes
Data
-phase rate
≤ 1 Mbit/s
up to 8 Mbit/s
Bit
-rate switch
No
Yes (BRS)
CRC length
15 bits
17 / 21 bits
Standard
ISO 11898
-1
ISO 11898
-
1:2015
FD controllers also speak classic CAN, easing migration on
mixed buses.
T H E F R O N T I E R
CAN XL & the Road Ahead
6
The CAN family keeps scaling to meet rising data demand while preserving its arbitration heritage.
Classic CAN
≤ 1 Mbit/s · 8 bytes
CAN FD
≤ 8 Mbit/s · 64 bytes
CAN XL
≤ 20 Mbit/s · 2048 bytes
■Bridging to Ethernet. CAN XL fills the gap between CAN FD and 100BASE-T1 automotive Ethernet.
■2,048-byte frames. Large payloads carry tunnelled higher-layer or IP-style data.
■Backward compatible. XL controllers still handle classic and FD traffic on the same wire.
■Zonal architectures. Future vehicles pair an Ethernet backbone with CAN at the sensor and actuator edge.
S Y S T E M A R C H I T E C T U R E
CAN in the Vehicle — Many Buses, One Gateway
7
A car never puts every ECU on one bus. Functional domains each
run their own CAN segment, joined by a central gateway.
■Domains, not one bus. Each domain runs its own CAN sized
for its load — so the ~32-node limit is never a problem.
■Central gateway. Routes and filters frames between domains,
translates bit rates and isolates faults.
■Mixed networks. CAN coexists with LIN sub-buses and
FlexRay / Ethernet under the same gateway.
■Diagnostics path. The OBD-II connector reaches every ECU
through the gateway.
GATEWAY
Powertrain CAN
500 kbit/s
Chassis CAN
500 kbit/s
Body CAN
125 kbit/s
Infotainment
CAN FD
N E T W O R K S E L E C T I O N
Which Bus? CAN vs. LIN, FlexRay & Ethernet
8
CAN is the cost-and-robustness sweet spot for control traffic; the others fill the ends of the spectrum.
Network
Typical rate
Access / topology
Cost
Where it fits
LIN
≤ 20 kbit/s
Single
-master sub-bus
$
Mirrors, seats, simple sensors
CAN / CAN FD
0.5
– 8 Mbit/s
Multi
-master bus
$$
Powertrain, body, chassis
FlexRay
10 Mbit/s
Time
-triggered, dual-channel
$$$
X
-by-wire, safety chassis
Automotive Ethernet
100 Mbit/s
– 1 Gbit/s+
Switched, point
-to-point
$$$
ADAS, cameras, zonal backbone
Rule of thumb: LIN where cost rules, FlexRay/Ethernet where bandwidth or hard determinism rules, and CAN for the deterministic, fault-
tolerant control traffic in between.
P R O D U C T I O N S O F T W A R E
From Bits to Signals — Stack & DBC
9
In a real ECU, application software never touches raw bits — a layered stack and a signal database sit in between.
THE AUTOSAR CAN STACK
Application / RTE Reads & writes named signals
COM · PDU Router Packs signals into PDUs and routes them
CanTp (ISO-TP) · CanNm Segments long data; network
management & sleep
CanIf · CanSM Hardware abstraction & bus-off recovery
CAN Driver · Transceiver Moves frames on and off the wire
THE SIGNAL LAYER (DBC / ARXML)
■Messages carry signals. One CAN frame packs several
signals into its data bytes.
■Byte order matters. Signals are laid out little-endian (Intel) or
big-endian (Motorola).
■Scaling & offset. Physical value = raw × factor + offset (e.g.
0.01 °C per bit).
■One source of truth. A .dbc or ARXML database defines
every message, signal and node.
EngineSpeed = raw × 0.25 rpm · little-endian · 2 bytes
C A N D ATA B A S E · D B C F I L E
Reading a DBC — Messages, Signals & Decoding
10
A DBC file is the single source of truth that maps raw CAN frames to named, scaled engineering values — every tool reads the same
definitions.
ANATOMY OF A .DBC FILE
BO_ 256 EngineData: 8 ENGINE_ECU
SG_ EngineSpeed : 0|16@1+ (0.25,0)
[0|16383] "rpm" Dashboard
SG_ CoolantTemp : 16|8@1+ (1,-40)
[-40|215] "degC" Dashboard
SG_ ThrottlePos : 24|8@1+ (0.4,0)
[0|100] "%" Engine_ECU
SIGNAL MAP ACROSS THE 8 DATA BYTES
B0 B1 B2 B3 B4 B5 B6 B7
EngineSpeed Coolan
t
Throttl
eUnused (bytes 4–7)
HOW TO READ A SIGNAL LINE
■BO_ = message. ID 256, name, byte count (8 = DLC) and the
sending ECU.
■SG_ = signal. start bit | length @ byte-order (1 = little-endian)
and sign (+).
■Factor & offset. (0.25, 0) scales raw counts into physical
units.
■Range & unit. [min|max] and "rpm" bound and label each
value.
EngineSpeed: raw 8000 × 0.25 + 0 = 2000 rpm
R E F E R E N C E
Glossary & Key Acronyms
11
A quick-reference card for the terms used throughout this deck.
Term
Meaning
SOF / EOF
Start / End of Frame delimiters
DLC
Data Length Code (payload size)
RTR / IDE
Remote request / Identifier
-extension bits
CRC
Cyclic Redundancy Check field
ACK
Acknowledgement slot
TEC / REC
Transmit / Receive Error Counters
Term
Meaning
CSMA/CR
Carrier
-sense access with collision resolution
DBC
CAN signal database (messages & signals)
ISO
-TP
ISO 15765
-2 transport for long data
UDS
Unified Diagnostic Services
SecOC
Secure Onboard Communication
ECU
Electronic Control Unit (a node)
